summary: Joel Spolsky dissects the click fraud problem plaguing Google's AdSense program. He explains the scam mechanics: fraudsters create fake blogs using scraped content, sign up for AdSense, then use networks of zombie PCs to simulate legitimate ad clicks and collect payments. He argues that this problem is inherent to any system that cuts checks based on clicks alone, drawing a parallel to the spectacularly failed AllAdvantage model from the first dot-com bubble. Spolsky distinguishes between AdSense fraud (where scammers profit directly) and the less severe AdWords fraud on Google's own site. He predicts Google will need to purge smaller AdSense sites en masse or risk destroying its reputation with advertisers.
key_points: Click fraud on AdSense follows a four-step playbook: create fake blogs from scraped content, sign up for AdSense, rent zombie PC networks, and simulate clicks from distributed IP addresses to appear legitimate
Any business model that pays affiliates based solely on clicks will inevitably attract fraud, as demonstrated by AllAdvantage's spectacular failure during the first dot-com bubble
AdWords (ads on Google's own site) is far less susceptible to fraud than AdSense because there's no direct way to profit from clicking those ads
Google's growth strategy depends on expanding ad inventory beyond its own search pages, making AdSense critical but also its biggest vulnerability
The long tail of millions of small sites makes fraud detection fundamentally harder than monitoring the top 1,000 legitimate publishers
money_quotes: The minute companies start cutting checks to 'affiliates' at the end of the month that are based on nothing more than clicks, you're bound to get the AllAdvantage phenomenon.
AllAdvantage was probably one of the most spectacularly stupid business ideas to come out of the first Dot Com bubble: a company that paid you to look at ads.

It is very hard to tell the difference between a fake blog and a real blog until you read it for a while and realize there's no human brain behind it, like one of those Jack Format radio stations that fired all their DJs, or maybe FEMA.
This is where Google is actually hurt by the long tail world of millions of small sites.

It's easy to evaluate the top 100 or 1000 web sites to make sure they're reasonably legit. It's much harder to monitor 1,000,000 blogs to make sure that none of them were machine generated for the purpose of scamming AdSense revenue.

I predict that you'll see a massive expulsion of smaller AdSense sites by Google, and it better happen soon, or AdSense will ruin Google's reputation among advertisers, something which could be deadly.
key_insight: Google's AdSense model contains a fundamental vulnerability: any system that pays affiliates per click will attract industrial-scale fraud, and the long tail of small publishers makes detection nearly impossible at scale.
